question.text: Scenario: Dr. Arun Patel, aged 50, is a senior physician earning Rs. 30 lakh per annum from his hospital practice. His wife Mrs. Shalini runs a small boutique earning Rs. 5 lakh per annum. They have one son, aged 22, who is completing his MBA and will be financially independent in 1 year. Dr. Patel wants to retire at 58. Their current financial position: Equity portfolio Rs. 45 lakh, Debt mutual funds Rs. 20 lakh, PPF Rs. 16 lakh (matures in 3 years), FDs Rs. 12 lakh, PF balance Rs. 25 lakh, Gold Rs. 10 lakh, Residential property (self-occupied) Rs. 1.2 crore. Liabilities: Home loan Rs. 15 lakh (EMI Rs. 22,000, 7 years remaining at 8%), no other loans. Monthly expenses: Rs. 1,80,000 (including EMI). Post-retirement, they estimate needing Rs. 1,20,000/month in today's terms. They want a retirement corpus that lasts 25 years post-retirement. Inflation is 6% p.a. Expected returns: equity 12%, debt 7%. Question 3 of 5: If post-retirement monthly expenses are Rs. 1,20,000 today and inflation is 6% p.a., what will the monthly expense be at the time of retirement (8 years later)?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Future monthly expense = 1,20,000 × (1.06)^8 = 1,20,000 × 1.59385 = Rs. 1,91,262 ≈ Rs. 1,91,232. **The Trap:** Rs. 1,80,000 uses simple inflation (1,20,000 × 1.5 = 1,80,000). Must use compound inflation. Rs. 2,00,000 is a round number estimate. **Cross-Exam Context:** Inflation-adjusted retirement expense calculation is central to X-A and XVII. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Most clients underestimate retirement expenses because they don't account for compounding inflation — Rs. 1.2 lakh today becomes Rs. 1.91 lakh in just 8 years at 6% inflation.
